item.title: Re: A compelling story with a context that alludes me.
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking not just the smell, but the feelings/associations as well. The disgust, revulsion and desperation experienced by those forced to eat the anchovies can be remotely applied to other people's silver holdings. Someone at MC&D fetching a silver ingot or silver anomalous objects would experience the same emotions/metal associations toward it as someone being forced to eat dried anchovies at gunpoint.

Otherwise, I don't see any point in forcing people to consume the anchovies under threat of violence, or any reason to integrate people into the machine at all.

item.title: A compelling story with a context that alludes me.
item.content:encoded:
I must admit, I quite enjoyed this read. The idea of channelling an abstract concept, to somehow weaponize is very interesting.

However, either by a major fault of myself, or a minor fault on the side of the author, I understand the chain of events as such:

M+C+D send the SCP the anomaly -> Smith is assigned -> Smith gets annoyed at the project? Develops a way to channel abstract concepts via an object/objects -> Scales this up for the SCP (or rather the silver bowl) -> He starts integrating humans into the mechanism somehow? But only when they eat the copies of the SCP? -> O5 love the end results so continue project, even though it means lots of deaths of personnel / d-class. -> The mind warping girl SCP gets slotted in there at one point?

The bold sections is where the plot is lost to me. I have 2 working theories:

- The focci is a combination of the anchovies and the silver, making all silver around the world smell like anchovies??? Hence causing M+C+D to abandon such silver assets and holdings.

-The focci is the silver itself, causing the foundation to be able to reproduce silver in the same way the anchovies do? Hence getting a monopoly on silver?

If it is a combination of the 2, neither, or 1 / the other is frankly irrelevant at this point. The point I am making is that I personally could not figure out that final piece of the context of the article.

As it is impossible to know , without personal input from the Author, if I have missed something critical / obvious, or the article itself lacks clear context in this matter, I am not going to up-vote or down-vote the article.

This is because it would be unfair to down-vote the article under the assumption the article is missing something, if in fact it is my own failure. Also, it would be equally unfair to up-vote the article in terms of it's quality and the competition under the assumption I have missed something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neither do I. It feels really disconnected — sugar bowls? The warning about discovering a cadaver?

It feels like a deliberate attempt to write something obtuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I don't get it. Plain and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what I just read, and I feel like even if I knew, I would find it dull. The only thing I understood was that there's a self-replicating dried anchovy. Has to be -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked how this captured my attention the whole way through but I'm honestly really stumped as to what the anomaly does and what happened in the story.

I read it over twice, and it seems like MCD gave duplicating anchovies (I don't get how they're thaumiel), and Smith was assigned to them. From my understanding, he goes crazy, blackmails his boss, kills all opposition to his PhD project, and then I'm totally lost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a contest entry…

As an entry in the Exquisite Corpse contest, this is a remarkable combined effort. I appreciate how incredibly complex and creative MaliceAforethought's SCProcs are (they're certainly the most involved Procedures I've come across thus far of the entries), and how the majority of it is seamlessly implemented in the article itself.

I've been eager to see how authors would adapt to working in the constraints of purely writing SCProcs, and this perfectly showcases the level of attention and effort I was looking forward to in this contest. It's also fitting that the tall order of this prompt is filled by a narrative which progressively gets more wild as it incorporates its many seemingly-disparate elements.

If I had any complaints, it would be that it doesn't seem like much of the second paragraph (SCProcs) made their way into the main article. While it's certainly still a significant portion of the overall SCProcs, it could be that I'm missing how it's inferred somewhere.

In all, if I were voting solely on articles' merit as contest entries, this would have my upvote.

Of course, this did have my upvote, but that's the subject of another post.

item.title: Really liked that one part where there's just intestines on the floor and there's not a lot of concern about it
item.content:encoded: The entire article was interesting and I found it oddly personal because I have the same last name of Silas ahah. I was genuinely surprised when Silas impaled the PO1-000 and enjoyed the circle back to the containment procedures. I felt unsettled by the lack of commentary from Silas, the innocuous nature of anchovies-yet still being involved with dead bodies-, and the nature of the unfinished chamber. Definitely a +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a really good one. Admittedly, I did feel like I was missing some background context to fully understand it, which probably is the case. I enjoyed it a lot regardless, though. For what it's worth, the article didn't feel overly long to me- I read the entirety in one sitting without any skimming, and it held my attention the entire time. Solid +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very conflicted here. On one hand, it's a bit long, and I found myself skimming for quite a few parts. On the other hand, I really like SCPs that start off as some innocuous BS and eventually unfold into an epic story. In addition, it amuses me to no end that the Foundation has a protocol for receiving "unexpected gifts" from Groups of Interest.

"Heh, gonna prank the Foundation by giving them a squirt gun that squirts lava! They won't be expecting this!"

…

"Hey, Jerry, we got another unmarked box—"

"Put it in the scan chamber. Jesus Christ, it's the fifth time this week. Do these people have nothing better to do?"

+1

item.title: Author
item.content:encoded:
You're fit to learn the proper meaning of a beatdown
Madness, chaos in the brain
Let my blood flow, make my blood flow through you, mane
You got no business questioning a thang
YOU'RE FIT TO LEARN THE PROPER MEANING OF A-

I like to say that all of my articles are incredibly personal, but I think this one is a bit beyond my other ones. As much as I like criticizing the systems around me, it's nice to look inward and rationalize the unpleasant things staring back at me.

+ Spoilers
...

Tag policy disallows the tagging of entities that are not directly referenced in the work proper. I never believed Mekhane was too much of a "naturally-occuring" god, anyways.

Thanks to N_Aepic_Fael and gee0765 for looking this over!

silverfish
fishfood
goremachine
